ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Sichuan water-boiled beef; 'beef dish' (FOODON_03315595) used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for this Sichuan preparation of boiled beef slices in chilli oil broth.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Shui Zhu Niu Rou; A0CKP (cooked beef) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to this specific Sichuan dish preparation.

preparation_method: Seasoned with Sichuan peppercorns and doubanjiang
main_ingredients: Beef (thinly sliced, typically flank or sirloin)
allergens: Gluten (soy sauce, doubanjiang, noodles if included)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Boiled Beef in Chilli Oil is approximately 700 g (one large bowl), providing roughly 1,100 kcal, 65 g protein, 80 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, flank, steak, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (FDC ID 174032) combined with component estimation for chilli oil, vegetables, and seasoning ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Shui Zhu Niu Rou as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef (FDC ID 174032) with component contributions from chilli oil, doubanjiang, soy sauce, vegetables (bean sprouts, cabbage), and cornstarch coating. The input record's per-100g values (energy 157 kcal, protein 9.3 g, fat 11.4 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained. Sodium at 400 mg/100g is plausible given doubanjiang and soy sauce content. All values cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy entries for individual components.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Mixed meat and vegetable dish with low-GI vegetables analogous estimate; University of Sydney GI Database entry for beef and vegetable composite dishes.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shui Zhu Niu Rou. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed beef and low-GI vegetable dishes (bean sprouts GI ~25, cabbage GI ~10, beef GI not applicable as protein/fat food). The small cornstarch coating and optional noodle component are the primary carbohydrate contributors; without noodles the dish is predominantly protein and fat with low-GI vegetables, supporting a GI of approximately 35. Glycemic load per 700 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 35.0) / 100 = 12.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; estimated 2,800 mg sodium per 700 g serving, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to very high sodium from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and chilli oil; if consumed, request reduced seasoning and limit portion to no more than 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.0 g/100 g) and low GI (~35) without noodles; however, high fat content (11.4 g/100 g) and large serving size (700 g) contribute significant caloric load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in moderation without noodles, as the low carbohydrate content and low GI minimise glycaemic impact, but should monitor total caloric intake given the high fat content and request reduced chilli oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (11.4 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.57 g/100 g), trans fat (0.07 g/100 g), cholesterol (25.71 mg/100 g), and very high sodium (400 mg/100 g) in a large 700 g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or consume only a small portion (≤150 g) due to the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and very high sodium, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6 pro-inflammatory pathway), chilli oil (AGE-generating), dried chillies (nightshade), and high sodium from doubanjiang
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the pro-inflammatory burden of red meat, chilli oil, and nightshade-derived chillies; consider substituting beef with tofu or chicken and requesting reduced chilli oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Beef in Chilli Oil (水煮牛肉, Shuǐ Zhǔ Niúròu) is a classic Sichuan Chinese dish featuring thinly sliced beef — typically flank or sirloin — that is briefly boiled or poached in a seasoned broth, then served over blanched low-GI vegetables such as bean sprouts and cabbage, and finished with a dramatic pour of sizzling hot chilli oil infused with Sichuan peppercorns, dried red chillies, garlic, and ginger. The dish is characterised by its intensely spicy, numbing (málà) flavour profile derived from the combination of dried chillies and Sichuan peppercorns, a rich and aromatic chilli oil broth, and tender, silky beef slices coated in a light cornstarch marinade that keeps the meat moist. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a large bowl (approximately 700 g) and may include wheat noodles or glass noodles. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 157 kcal, 9.3 g protein, 11.4 g fat (3.6 g saturated), 5.0 g carbohydrate, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content from doubanjiang (fermented broad bean paste) and soy sauce makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~35) make it relatively suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without noodles and in controlled portions. The red meat and chilli oil content raise concerns for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid and AGE-generating hot oil.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Shui Zhu Niu Rou as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef with component contributions from chilli oil, doubanjiang, soy sauce, and vegetables. The input record's values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed beef and low-GI vegetable dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Sichuan dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2026. Values were estimated from individual component USDA SR Legacy entries; detailed micronutrient breakdown was not derivable with sufficient confidence to report positive values. All core macros (protein_g 9.29, fat_g 11.43, carbohydrate_g 5.0) are non-zero and sourced from USDA FoodData Central component estimation; no zero-macro justification is required. Trans fat (0.07 g/100g) is a trace amount consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant beef fat. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Shui Zhu Niu Rou has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains beef which may not be halal-certified in all Singapore restaurant contexts; consumers should verify with the specific establishment.
